product-brief.mdprfaq-{project}.md
OUT
PRD.md
02 · B
UX Design
Two-spine rewrite: DESIGN.md (visual tokens) + EXPERIENCE.md (behavior/flow/IA), cross-referenced via {path.to.token}. Adds named-protagonist journeys, surface-closure validation, opt-in reviewer gate, producer-handoff registry (default Stitch). Six-step activation.
managed byUX Expert
  1. 01
    Load PRD & context
    Read PRD, understand users, FRs and NFRs relevant to UX.
    requirements analysis
    what

    AI loads PRD and extracts UX-relevant FRs + NFRs before starting design.

    • 1
      AI loads prd.md, extracts personas + FRs with UI surface
    • 2
      Identify NFRs that constrain UX
      • Performance — p95 <200ms rules out heavy animations
      • Accessibility — WCAG 2.1 AA requires focus states, contrast ratios
      • Responsive — mobile-first if mobile traffic >50%
    • 3
      Output: FR → UX implication table (e.g.: FR-03 'user uploads file' → drag-drop + progress + error handling)
    why
    • ·
      UX is not separate from requirements — NFR constraints rule out certain design patterns
    • ·
      Starting with requirements load prevents sketching wireframes that need to be revised
    • ·
      Performance NFR in particular: 200ms threshold rules out long skeleton screens
    how
    • ·
      Requirements traceability: FR → UX needs mapping table
    • ·
      AI highlights NFRs that constrain design (mark with ⚠️ in notes)
    • ·
      Personas extracted → primary persona drives primary flow
  2. 02
    User journey mapping
    Map the journey for each persona — touchpoints, emotions, pain points.
    journey mappingJTBD
    what

    AI maps user journey for each persona: touchpoints, emotions, pain points, opportunities.

    • 1
      Journey map 5 stages
      • Awareness — where users hear about product
      • Consideration — evaluation, comparison
      • Onboarding — signup → first meaningful action
      • Regular use — habitual engagement pattern
      • Advocacy — retention, referral, renewal
    • 2
      Each stage: touchpoints + emotion (frustrated/curious/delighted) + pain + opportunity
    • 3
      Flag moments of truth — transitions where users most likely to drop off
    why
    • ·
      Static screens hide flow — journey reveals transitions where UX problems live
    • ·
      Most dropoff happens at transitions, not at the screens themselves
    • ·
      Peak-end rule: design peak delight moment + strong end moment
    how
    • ·
      Journey mapping methodology with emotion curve
    • ·
      Transitions tagged with 'danger level' — high = prioritize design attention
    • ·
      One journey per persona — don't merge (different people, different paths)
  3. 03
    Information architecture
    Information structure, navigation, sitemap, content hierarchy.
    card sortingsitemap
    what

    AI define Information Architecture: sitemap, navigation hierarchy, content groupings.

    • 1
      Output of this step
      • Sitemap — all screens/pages and relationships
      • Navigation structure — top-nav / sidebar / breadcrumb decision
      • Content taxonomy — categories, labels, search terms
    • 2
      AI uses card sorting to discover user's mental model
    • 3
      Validate: can the user find feature X in ≤3 clicks?
    why
    • ·
      Bad IA = user gets lost even when the product has enough features
    • ·
      Research: if users can't find it in 3 clicks, they assume the feature doesn't exist
    • ·
      Defining IA upfront = no costly rework after mockups are polished
    how
    • ·
      Card sorting: open (user groups) or closed (user fits into pre-set)
    • ·
      Tree testing: validate IA with real tasks
    • ·
      Output: sitemap diagram + nav structure decision rationale
  4. 04
    Wireframes (low-fi)
    Sketch the main screens with basic layout and structure.
    low-fi prototyping
    what

    AI sketches low-fidelity wireframes for 5-10 main screens — gray boxes, no color.

    • 1
      Mobile-first: sketch mobile viewport first, desktop after
    • 2
      Gray rectangles + placeholder text — focus on layout + hierarchy, not aesthetics
    • 3
      Per screen annotations
      • Primary action — most important thing user does here
      • Navigation — entry/exit points
      • Content blocks — type, priority, size estimate
    why
    • ·
      Lo-fi because high-fi too early = team debates colors instead of debating flow
    • ·
      Lo-fi cheap to discard — wrong → fix in 30 minutes, not 3 days
    • ·
      Mobile-first reveals priorities: limited space require what's actually important
    how
    • ·
      Crazy 8s technique: sketch 8 variations in 8 minutes before committing
    • ·
      Gray only — no brand colors, no real images, no real text
    • ·
      Annotation mandatory — no annotation = design intent cannot be shared
  5. 05
    Interaction patterns
    Define patterns (form, search, filter, modal...) and micro-interactions.
    interaction designmicro-interactions
    what

    AI define interaction patterns — standardize behavior across product.

    • 1
      Core patterns to define
      • Forms — validation timing (on-blur vs on-submit), error display, inline vs summary
      • Search — autocomplete, filters, results pagination
      • Modals — when to use, dismissible via ESC/backdrop, focus trap
      • Notifications — toast / inline / system / email trigger conditions
      • Loading states — skeleton / spinner / progress bar — when each
    • 2
      Each pattern: trigger condition + behavior + edge cases
    why
    • ·
      Pattern reuse = consistency — predictability builds trust
    • ·
      Define once → all forms behave same way; user learns once, applies everywhere
    • ·
      Inconsistent patterns = cognitive load on every screen the user must re-learn
    how
    • ·
      Reference Material Design / Apple HIG / Carbon for industry-standard patterns
    • ·
      Custom patterns only when the product has a strong reason — document rationale
    • ·
      Pattern library output feeds developer component specs in step 06
  6. 06
    Component specs
    Describe component types, states, and variants — ready for dev.
    atomic design
    what

    AI lists component specs — each component has states + variants + behavior.

    • 1
      Atom-level components per Atomic Design
      • Atoms — Button, Input, Label, Icon, Badge
      • Molecules — FormField, SearchBar, Card, MenuItem
      • Organisms — Header, DataTable, Modal, Sidebar
    • 2
      Each component spec
      • States — default / hover / active / disabled / error / loading
      • Variants — primary / secondary / destructive / ghost
      • Accessibility — ARIA role, keyboard, focus visible
    why
    • ·
      Component spec is the contract between UX and Dev
    • ·
      Underspec → dev guess → inconsistency across screens
    • ·
      Atomic Design (Brad Frost): build small, compose large — reusable and maintainable
    how
    • ·
      Atomic Design methodology: atoms → molecules → organisms → templates → pages
    • ·
      Each component: visual spec + behavior spec + a11y spec + code stub
    • ·
      Export to Figma component library if the team uses Figma
  7. 07
    Responsive design
    Breakpoints, mobile/tablet/desktop behaviors, adaptive patterns.
    mobile-firstprogressive enhancement
    what

    AI define responsive behavior: breakpoints + per-breakpoint layout decisions.

    • 1
      Standard breakpoints
      • Mobile — <640px: single column, bottom nav
      • Tablet — 640-1024px: 2 column, side nav optional
      • Desktop — >1024px: multi-column, expanded nav
    • 2
      Per-breakpoint decisions
      • Navigation pattern changes (bottom nav → side nav → top nav)
      • Content priority (mobile hides secondary actions)
      • Component reflow (card stack vs grid)
    why
    • ·
      70% web traffic mobile — mobile-first is not an afterthought
    • ·
      Mobile-first vs desktop-first determines downstream code structure
    • ·
      Explicit breakpoints prevent devs from picking random values (768? 800? 1024?)
    how
    • ·
      Progressive enhancement: start mobile, add capabilities for larger screens
    • ·
      Content priority per breakpoint: what shows, what hides, what reflows
    • ·
      Test on real devices — DevTools doesn't capture touch behavior
  8. 08
    Accessibility
    WCAG 2.1 AA: keyboard nav, screen reader, contrast, focus states.
    WCAG 2.1ARIA
    what

    AI specifies accessibility per WCAG 2.1 AA — not a nice-to-have.

    • 1
      Core WCAG 2.1 AA requirements
      • Contrast — 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text
      • Keyboard nav — all interactive elements reachable via Tab
      • Screen reader — meaningful labels, alt text, ARIA where needed
      • Focus visible — clear focus indicator on all elements
      • Error identification — errors described in text, not only color
      • Motion — prefers-reduced-motion respected
    why
    • ·
      Legal requirement in many markets: ADA (US), EAA (EU 2025)
    • ·
      Cost of retrofit 10x cost of build-in
    • ·
      Accessibility improvements help all users — SEO, keyboard power users, mobile
    how
    • ·
      WCAG 2.1 AA checklist + ARIA patterns
    • ·
      Tools: aXe DevTools, Lighthouse a11y audit
    • ·
      Manual test: keyboard-only navigation + screen reader (NVDA/VoiceOver)
  9. 09
    Design system references
    Link to design system / tokens, or propose creating one.
    design tokens
    what

    AI references or proposes building a design system with semantic tokens.

    • 1
      Design tokens — W3C standard
      • Color — semantic naming: color.bg.primary not color.blue.500
      • Typography — scale: xs/sm/md/lg/xl, line-heights, weights
      • Spacing — 4px base grid, named scale: 1/2/3/4/6/8/12/16...
      • Shadow, radius, motion — consistent values
    • 2
      1 token change propagates everywhere — brand refresh = update 20 tokens, not 200 screens
    why
    • ·
      Without design system: every screen reinvents wheels
    • ·
      Token-based approach lets 1 change propagate everywhere
    • ·
      Critical for Level 3-4 projects with multiple feature teams
    how
    • ·
      W3C Design Tokens standard format
    • ·
      Reference: Tailwind, Material, Carbon, Polaris — pick closest to team's stack
    • ·
      Semantic naming: describes role, not appearance
  10. 10
    Finalize ux-spec
    Save DESIGN.md + EXPERIENCE.md, handoff to Architecture.
    specification
    what

    AI saves DESIGN.md + EXPERIENCE.md and clearly hands off to Architecture.

    • 1
      Document structure
      • User journeys — per persona
      • Information Architecture — sitemap + nav structure
      • Wireframes — embedded or linked
      • Interaction patterns — pattern library
      • Component specs — states + variants
      • Responsive behavior — breakpoint table
      • Accessibility — WCAG checklist
      • Design system — tokens reference
    • 2
      AI invoke bmad-help → suggest Architecture (Phase 3) next
    why
    • ·
      UX-spec is the input for Architecture — component list informs frontend framework choice
    • ·
      Accessibility spec informs library choice (a11y-first vs not)
    • ·
      Interaction patterns inform state management approach
    how
    • ·
      Handoff format standard BMAD — frontmatter complete with stepsCompleted
    • ·
      Wireframes: embed as ASCII/text description if Figma is not available
    • ·
      bmad-help arg='ux-design' → recommend next: architecture
Skills
bmad-uxbmad-mermaid-generate
Frameworks
User Journey MappingInformation ArchitectureWireframingWCAG 2.1 AAResponsive breakpointsDesign Systems (Material 3 · HIG · Carbon · Polaris · Tailwind)

IN
PRD.md

03
Solutioning
Decide how to build it · Break work down into stories
Required
03 · A
Architecture
Intent-routed (Create / Update / Validate across 5 entry shapes) — ARCHITECTURE-SPINE.md is the single source of truth. Breadth-coverage rubric, optional reviewer gate, headless mode supported.
managed byArchitect

Steps below reflect the Create intent's flow (one of 5 entry shapes: Create / Update / Validate against an existing spine / headless). Update and Validate intents reuse the same driver → ADR framework at a lighter scope.

  1. 01
    Init & load PRD
    Read PRD and UX, identify the most important architecture drivers.
    requirements traceability
    what

    AI loads PRD + ux-spec and identifies top architecture drivers.

    • 1
      AI extracts from PRD
      • Top 3-5 NFRs with highest architectural impact
      • Key FRs with high architectural coupling (e.g.: real-time sync, file upload, payments)
      • Compliance constraints from step 05 of the PRD
    • 2
      Rank drivers: performance / security / scalability / maintainability
    • 3
      Output: driver list with justification — this is the 'north star' for all subsequent decisions
    why
    • ·
      Not all NFRs are equal — 'fast' and '99.99% uptime' have completely different implications
    • ·
      Top 3-5 drivers focus architecture effort, avoiding over-engineering on things that don't matter
    • ·
      Without explicit drivers, team debates style preference instead of tradeoffs
    how
    • ·
      Quality attribute taxonomy: performance / availability / security / modifiability / testability
    • ·
      Drivers ranked by business impact if violated
    • ·
      ATAM (Architecture Tradeoff Analysis Method) light-version
  2. 02
    High-level architecture
    Draw the overall system diagram, identify components and boundaries.
    C4 modelsystem design
    what

    AI draws high-level system architecture per C4 model.

    • 1
      C4 model — 4 levels
      • Context diagram — system boundary + external actors (users, 3rd party systems)
      • Container diagram — major deployable units (web app, API, DB, queue, CDN)
      • Component diagram — major components within each container
      • Code diagram — optional, only for critical paths
    • 2
      Sweet spot: Container diagram is most useful for team alignment
    • 3
      Annotate data flows with: protocol + auth method + sync/async
    why
    • ·
      C4 Context = for all stakeholders (including non-tech) to understand system boundaries
    • ·
      C4 Container = for engineers to understand major building blocks and dependencies
    • ·
      Layered abstraction: dig down only when needed — not overwhelming
    how
    • ·
      C4 model by Simon Brown — text-based (PlantUML/Mermaid) or diagram
    • ·
      Annotations mandatory: unlabeled arrows = ambiguous coupling
    • ·
      Container diagram stable through refactors — code changes but containers rarely change
  3. 03
    Tech stack decision
    Choose language, framework, DB, infra — record ADRs (Architecture Decision Records).
    ADRtrade-off analysis
    what

    AI decides tech stack — each major decision is 1 ADR.

    • 1
      Standard ADR format (Michael Nygard)
      • Title — 'ADR-001: Use PostgreSQL for primary data store'
      • Status — Proposed / Accepted / Superseded
      • Context — what problem are we solving
      • Decision — what we decided
      • Consequences — tradeoffs accepted
      • Alternatives — what we rejected and why
    • 2
      Each major decision: language, framework, database, cache, queue, infra, observability
    why
    • ·
      ADRs record WHY, not just what — 6 months later the team won't re-debate
    • ·
      'Because the team knows it' is context, not a decision reason
    • ·
      Documented alternatives prevent future revisiting of rejected options
    how
    • ·
      Trade-off analysis: score alternatives per NFR drivers
    • ·
      ADR files: 1 file per decision, immutable (new decision = new ADR)
    • ·
      ADRs/ADR-001-database.md naming convention
  4. 04
    Data model
    Design entities, relationships, schema, migration strategy.
    ERDevent sourcing
    what

    AI design data model: entities, relationships, schema, indexes, migration strategy.

    • 1
      Data model artifacts
      • Entity list — major business objects (User, Order, Product...)
      • ERD — relationships: 1-1, 1-N, N-M
      • Schema — key fields, types, constraints, indexes
      • Sensitive data — PII fields tagged, encryption strategy
    • 2
      Migration strategy
      • Zero-downtime: expand-contract pattern (blue-green migrations)
      • Maintenance window: simpler but requires downtime
      • Decision depends on SLA NFRs
    why
    • ·
      Data model is the most expensive thing to change after ship — front-load
    • ·
      Schema design surfaces implicit business rules the PRD didn't articulate ('Can a User have multiple Addresses?')
    • ·
      Migration strategy must be decided upfront — retrofitting zero-downtime is very complex
    how
    • ·
      ERD diagram for relational; event sourcing if audit + time-travel is needed
    • ·
      Index strategy: query-driven (YAGNI — don't add indexes speculatively)
    • ·
      PII handling: encrypt at rest, pseudonymize in logs, access control
  5. 05
    API design
    REST/GraphQL/gRPC, endpoints, authentication, versioning, contracts.
    RESTOpenAPI
    what

    AI define API design: style, endpoints, auth, versioning, contracts.

    • 1
      API style decision
      • REST — resource-oriented, stateless, good for CRUD
      • GraphQL — flexible queries, good for complex data graphs
      • gRPC — binary, streaming, good for internal microservices
      • Event-driven — async, good for decoupling and scale
    • 2
      Auth strategy: OAuth 2.0 / API keys / JWT — pick based on client types
    • 3
      Contract artifacts
      • OpenAPI 3.0 spec for REST
      • SDL (Schema Definition Language) for GraphQL
      • .proto files for gRPC
    why
    • ·
      API contract is the most-coupled interface — clients depend on shape
    • ·
      REST vs GraphQL mismatch between epics = integration nightmare (per Why Solutioning doc)
    • ·
      Versioning strategy: decide once, all teams follow — URL path vs header
    how
    • ·
      REST best practices: resource-oriented, proper HTTP verbs, consistent naming
    • ·
      Versioning: URL path (/v1/) simpler and more visible
    • ·
      Contract-first: write OpenAPI/SDL before writing code
  6. 06
    Integration patterns
    External services, message queues, webhooks, event patterns.
    event-drivenhexagonal
    what

    AI defines integration patterns for external services.

    • 1
      Each integration must specify
      • Protocol — REST / webhook / event bus / SDK
      • Auth — API key / OAuth / mTLS
      • Failure handling — timeout + retry policy + fallback
      • Rate limiting — throttle strategy
    • 2
      Resilience patterns
      • Circuit Breaker — fail fast when service is down
      • Retry with backoff — exponential, with jitter
      • Dead Letter Queue — catch failed messages
    why
    • ·
      Each integration = a new failure mode (3rd party downtime, rate limits, breaking changes)
    • ·
      Circuit breaker prevents cascade failures — critical for high-availability systems
    • ·
      Without retry + DLQ, transient failures = permanent data loss
    how
    • ·
      Hexagonal architecture (ports & adapters) to isolate external dependencies
    • ·
      Specify timeout/retry/fallback for each integration explicitly
    • ·
      Vendor lock-in assessment: what's the cost of switching?
  7. 07
    Security architecture
    Authn/authz, encryption, secrets management, threat model.
    threat modelingSTRIDE
    what

    AI specifies security architecture using the STRIDE threat model.

    • 1
      Auth + AuthZ stack
      • Authentication — OAuth 2.0 / SAML / JWT / OIDC
      • Authorization — RBAC (role-based) / ABAC (attribute-based)
      • Session management — token lifetime, refresh strategy
    • 2
      Data security
      • Encryption at rest — AES-256 for sensitive data
      • Encryption in transit — TLS 1.3 minimum
      • Secrets management — Vault / AWS Secrets Manager / env vars anti-pattern
    • 3
      STRIDE threat model per component
      • Spoofing / Tampering / Repudiation / Info Disclosure / DoS / Elevation
    why
    • ·
      Security retrofit cost 10-100x build-in
    • ·
      Early threat modeling surfaces hidden attack surfaces
    • ·
      Compliance (SOC2, ISO 27001) requires documented security architecture
    how
    • ·
      STRIDE per component — systematic coverage
    • ·
      OWASP Top 10 checklist minimum
    • ·
      Secrets in code = automatic fail — use secret manager from day 1
  8. 08
    Deployment architecture
    Cloud topology, environments, CI/CD, observability (logs, metrics, traces).
    12-factorobservability
    what

    AI define deployment architecture: cloud topology + CI/CD + observability.

    • 1
      Infrastructure
      • Cloud topology — regions, AZs, CDN strategy
      • Environments — dev / staging / prod isolation
      • Scaling — horizontal vs vertical, auto-scaling triggers
    • 2
      Observability triad
      • Logs — structured (JSON), centralized, retention policy
      • Metrics — system (CPU/RAM/latency) + business (DAU, revenue)
      • Traces — distributed tracing for request flows
    • 3
      CI/CD pipeline
      • Build → Test → Security scan → Deploy → Smoke test
      • Rollback strategy: blue-green or feature flags
    why
    • ·
      Production failures invisible without observability
    • ·
      Logs = what happened; Metrics = is system healthy; Traces = why slow
    • ·
      All 3 pillars needed before shipping — retrofitting observability is painful
    how
    • ·
      12-factor app principles
    • ·
      OpenTelemetry standard for tracing — vendor-neutral
    • ·
      Observability triad per Cindy Sridharan 'Distributed Systems Observability'
  9. 09
    NFR validation
    Map each NFR to an architectural solution, prove achievability.
    ATAMquality scenarios
    what

    AI maps each NFR → architectural solution, proves coverage qualitatively.

    • 1
      NFR validation table
      • NFR — description with metric
      • Architecture solution — which component/pattern addresses it
      • Quantified expectation — calculation or reasoning
      • Validation method — load test / chaos test / audit
    • 2
      AI writes 'NFR-01 p95 <200ms → read replica + CDN + Redis cache → expected ~150ms'
    why
    • ·
      Architecture must close-loop with NFRs — do not assume coverage
    • ·
      Without explicit mapping, NFRs become an afterthought discovered only at failure
    • ·
      Quantified expectation enables later verification with actual load tests
    how
    • ·
      ATAM quality scenarios: stimulus / response / measure
    • ·
      Acceptable tradeoffs documented (e.g.: 'traded 20ms latency for stronger consistency')
    • ·
      Gaps surface: NFRs without an architectural solution = must address before Phase 4
  10. 10
    Risk analysis
    Technical risks, scaling bottlenecks, vendor lock-in, mitigation plans.
    failure mode analysis
    what

    AI lists technical risks with FMEA scoring.

    • 1
      Each risk has
      • Description — what could go wrong
      • Category — scaling / vendor / skill / deprecation / security
      • FMEA score — Severity × Occurrence × Detection
      • Mitigation — action + owner
      • Monitoring trigger — early warning signal
    why
    • ·
      Architecture risks different business risks — surface only via technical analysis
    • ·
      Unknown unknowns only appear under load or vendor pivot — identify early
    • ·
      Front-loading identification reduces 80% of bad surprises in Phase 4
    how
    • ·
      FMEA scoring: Severity (1-10) × Occurrence (1-10) × Detection (1-10)
    • ·
      Top 5 risks by score get mitigation plans
    • ·
      Failure mode analysis: 'What fails first under 10x load?'
    • ·
      Each risk has an owner + trigger condition — know exactly when risk becomes a real issue
  11. 11
    Finalize + ADRs
    Save ARCHITECTURE-SPINE.md + all ADRs, recommend Epics & Stories.
    ADR documentation
    what

    AI saves ARCHITECTURE-SPINE.md + ADRs/*.md and hands off.

    • 1
      Output files
      • ARCHITECTURE-SPINE.md — C4 diagrams + tech stack + data model + API design + security + deployment
      • ADRs/*.md — 1 file per major decision, numbered
      • project-context.md — 'constitution' for AI agents: patterns, conventions, do/don't
    • 2
      ADRs are immutable: new decision = new ADR (don't edit old ones)
    • 3
      AI invoke bmad-help → suggest Epics & Stories (03·B) next
    why
    • ·
      Architecture shipped as artifact (not just discussion) = single source of truth
    • ·
      ADRs immutable: record decision history, each new decision = new ADR
    • ·
      project-context.md propagates conventions to all dev agents downstream
    how
    • ·
      ADR format: numbered, status field (Proposed/Accepted/Superseded), supersedes link
    • ·
      project-context.md sections: tech stack summary, code patterns, naming conventions, anti-patterns
    • ·
      bmad-help auto-invoke — uniform handoff pattern
Skills
bmad-architecturebmad-mermaid-generate
Frameworks
C4 ModelADR (Nygard)STRIDE12-FactorATAMFMEAReference-class forecasting
Output Formats
MarkdownADRs/*.md (immutable)project-context.mdMermaid (C4/ERD/sequence)
Tools · MCP
advanced-elicitationparty-modebmad-help
IN
PRD.mdDESIGN.mdEXPERIENCE.md
OUT
ARCHITECTURE-SPINE.mdADRs/*.md
03 · B
Epics & Stories
Created AFTER architecture (v6.8) — stories reflect actual technical decisions. 4-step micro-file: validate prerequisites → design epics → generate stories → final validation. UX-DRs are first-class input. JIT entity creation.
managed byPM + Architect
  1. 01
    Load PRD + architecture
    Read both to understand requirements and existing technical decisions.
    traceability
    what

    AI loads PRD + ARCHITECTURE-SPINE.md and builds FR ↔ component traceability map.

    • 1
      AI loads both documents in parallel
      • prd.md — FRs, NFRs, personas, success metrics
      • ARCHITECTURE-SPINE.md — components, data model, API design, patterns
    • 2
      Build traceability matrix
      • Rows = FRs from PRD
      • Cols = architecture components
      • X = 'this component addresses this FR'
    • 3
      Flag gaps: FR with no component (architecture incomplete) + component with no FR (over-engineering)
    why
    • ·
      v6 improvement: stories created AFTER architecture — stories now reference specific patterns
    • ·
      Stories created before architecture miss tech context → rework mid-sprint
    • ·
      Traceability reveals gaps BEFORE committing sprint capacity
    how
    • ·
      Traceability matrix: rows = FRs, cols = components, X = mapping
    • ·
      Empty row = FR uncovered → must address in architecture
    • ·
      Empty col = component not justified → over-engineering, remove or justify
  2. 02
    Group FRs into epics
    Group related FRs into epics — each epic delivers independent value.
    capability mapping
    what

    AI groups FRs into epics — each epic is 1 user-deliverable capability.

    • 1
      AI clusters FRs by user capability (not by tech layer)
    • 2
      Validate each epic
      • User-deliverable: can it be demoed standalone?
      • Independent value: do users benefit if only this epic ships?
      • Reasonable scope: 2-6 weeks development?
    • 3
      Bad vs good epic naming
      • Bad: 'Backend', 'Frontend' — tech layers, not capabilities
      • Good: 'User onboarding', 'Payment processing', 'Admin dashboard'
    why
    • ·
      Tech-layer epics don't deliver value individually
    • ·
      User-centric epics deliver value each time an epic ships — stakeholders see progress
    • ·
      Validate 'demo standalone' is the acid test for epic independence
    how
    • ·
      Capability mapping: each FR assigned to exactly 1 epic
    • ·
      Epic size check: 1 epic too large (>6 weeks) → split; too small (<1 week) → merge
    • ·
      Validate with product vision: each epic is 1 capability story you can tell
  3. 03
    Epic ordering
    Determine epic order by dependencies, risk, and value.
    WSJFvalue stream
    what

    AI order epics per WSJF (Weighted Shortest Job First).

    • 1
      WSJF scoring for each epic
      • User-Business Value — direct revenue/UX impact (1-10)
      • Time Criticality — cost of delaying (1-10)
      • Risk Reduction — reduces unknowns if done early (1-10)
      • Job Size — estimated weeks (1-10, higher = larger)
      • WSJF = (Value + Criticality + Risk) / Size
    • 2
      High-risk + high-value epics → ship early (fail fast while there is still runway)
    • 3
      First epic must independent (no dependencies) — unblock parallel work
    why
    • ·
      Shipping in wrong order = waste — first 4 sprints don't deliver demoable value = stakeholder frustration
    • ·
      Risky epics early: if they fail → pivot while still having runway
    • ·
      Value-first: demonstrate value early → stakeholder confidence + feedback
    how
    • ·
      SAFe WSJF framework
    • ·
      Score subjectively if no exact data — relative ranking is sufficient
    • ·
      Visualize: table with scores + bar chart for stakeholders to see rationale
  4. 04
    Story breakdown
    Break epics into stories per INVEST principles.
    INVESTSPIDR
    what

    AI breaks each epic into stories per INVEST.

    • 1
      INVEST checklist for each story
      • Independent — don't block each other (when possible)
      • Negotiable — scope can be adjusted
      • Valuable — user/business benefit is clear
      • Estimable — effort can be estimated
      • Small — 1-3 days dev work ideal
      • Testable — AC can be verified
    • 2
      SPIDR splitting patterns when story is too large
      • Spike — separate exploration/research story
      • Path — split by user journey paths
      • Interface — split by UI vs API vs integration
      • Data — split by data variations
      • Rules — split by business rules
    why
    • ·
      Story too large → uncertainty → wrong estimate → sprint slippage
    • ·
      Story too small → overhead (PR review, deploy) > value
    • ·
      INVEST sweet spot: 1-3 days reduces risk and enables fast feedback
    how
    • ·
      INVEST checklist per story — fail any = must split/reshape
    • ·
      SPIDR patterns: most used = Interface split (API vs UI separate stories)
    • ·
      Story size: count by AC — story with >7 AC is usually too large, split
  5. 05
    Acceptance criteria
    Write AC per Given/When/Then for each story, detailed enough to test.
    BDDGherkin
    what

    AI writes Acceptance Criteria per BDD/Gherkin — executable specification.

    • 1
      Gherkin format
      • Given — precondition (state before the action)
      • When — action user takes
      • Then — expected outcome (verifiable)
    • 2
      Coverage for each story
      • 1+ AC for happy path
      • 1-2 edge cases (boundary values, empty states)
      • 1 error path (what happens when it fails)
    • 3
      3-7 AC per story — below 3 = insufficient spec; above 7 = story too large
    why
    • ·
      Vague AC ('works correctly') → endless debate about done
    • ·
      BDD format forces precondition + trigger + outcome — won't miss edge cases
    • ·
      Executable AC: if using Cucumber/Playwright, tests write themselves from AC
    how
    • ·
      Each AC independently testable — don't chain multiple outcomes into 1 AC
    • ·
      Edge cases from INVEST size estimate: 'what are the boundaries of this story?'
    • ·
      Error paths mandatory: 'What does the user see when it fails?'
  6. 06
    Story dependencies
    Identify dependencies between stories, mark blockers.
    dependency graph
    what

    AI builds dependency graph between stories.

    • 1
      3 types of dependency
      • Hard blocker — story B cannot start if A is not done
      • Soft dependency — B is easier if A is done, but B can start
      • Independent — no dependency
    • 2
      AI identifies critical path — the longest chain of blockers
    • 3
      Flag stories with >3 dependencies — signal to split or re-sequence
    why
    • ·
      Dependency graph drives sprint planning and parallel work
    • ·
      Critical path = minimum sprint duration — visualize for SM
    • ·
      Unresolved dependencies = sprint blocker surprise
    how
    • ·
      Build graph: nodes = stories, directed edges = blocker
    • ·
      Critical path analysis: longest path = minimum sprint duration
    • ·
      Parallel opportunities: stories with no outgoing dependencies = safe to parallelize
    • ·
      Cross-epic dependency = red flag: story depending on another epic breaks sprint boundary
  7. 07
    Finalize epic files
    Save epic-{N}.md for each epic with all stories inside.
    backlog
    what

    AI saves epic-{N}.md per epic — stories nested inside.

    • 1
      File structure
      • 1 file per epic: epic-01-user-onboarding.md
      • Frontmatter: epic ID, status, owner, WSJF score
      • Stories inside: S1, S2, S3... with full AC and tech context
    • 2
      Sharding for large epics
      • Option: stories/epic-01/story-01.md separated
      • Use when epic >10 stories — avoid one huge file
    • 3
      AI invoke bmad-help → suggest Readiness Check (03·C) next
    why
    • ·
      1 file 1 epic = just-in-time loading — Phase 4 loads only 1 epic at a time
    • ·
      Full story content in epic file = dev agent reads epic only, no need for PRD/architecture
    • ·
      Separation ensures minimal context window per sprint
    how
    • ·
      Frontmatter: epicId, status, owner, sequence, wsjfScore
    • ·
      Stories numbered S1, S2 within epic — globally unique via epic-01-S1
    • ·
      bmad-help recommend: Readiness Check (03·C) before Phase 4
Skills
bmad-create-epics-and-stories
Frameworks
INVESTStory SlicingAcceptance CriteriaDependency MappingEpic Ordering
Output Formats
Markdown epic-{N}.mdstories/*.md
Tools · MCP
party-mode (PM+Architect)bmad-help
IN
PRD.mdARCHITECTURE-SPINE.md
OUT
epic-{N}.mdstories/*.md
03 · C
Readiness Check
Gate check (v6.8) — 6-step sequential. Step 1 interactive only; Steps 2–6 auto. Validates FR coverage, UX alignment, epic quality. Verdict: READY / NEEDS WORK / NOT READY. Severity: Critical / Major / Minor.
managed byPM
  1. 01
    Load planning docs
    Read PRD, architecture, epics, ux-spec — collect all planning artifacts.
    document review
    what

    PO agent loads all planning artifacts and builds inventory.

    • 1
      Load documents
      • prd.md + addendum.md + decision-log.md
      • ARCHITECTURE-SPINE.md + ADRs/*.md
      • epic-{N}.md all epics
      • DESIGN.md + EXPERIENCE.md if available
    • 2
      PO builds artifact inventory: file list + version + last-updated
    • 3
      Flag missing artifacts before starting review
    why
    • ·
      Readiness check is the gatekeeper before Phase 4
    • ·
      PO ≠ PM — independent reviewer reduces self-confirmation bias
    • ·
      BMAD tip: use different LLM for validation — same LLM finds own writing consistent
    how
    • ·
      PO profile: skeptical, thorough, user-advocate perspective
    • ·
      Artifact inventory: date comparison (architecture newer than PRD = check for drift)
    • ·
      Missing artifacts = automatic Concerns flag
    • ·
      Version pin: record commit/hash of each doc to ensure reviewing the correct version
  2. 02
    PRD quality review
    Check completeness, clarity, FRs/NFRs are complete and non-contradictory.
    completeness check
    what

    AI review PRD: completeness + consistency + FR quality.

    • 1
      Completeness check
      • Every required section present and non-empty?
      • Every FR has ID + title + description + priority + user story?
      • Every NFR has metric + threshold + measurement method?
      • Exec summary present and covers all major points?
    • 2
      Consistency check
      • Exec summary match body detail?
      • FR-IDs referenced consistently (FR-03 in one place, FR-3 elsewhere = error)?
      • NFRs don't contradict FRs?
    why
    • ·
      PRD bugs are cheapest to fix early — propagates downstream to architecture and stories
    • ·
      1 missing FR = 1 missing feature = customer complaint
    • ·
      1 vague NFR = battle between dev and stakeholder post-ship
    how
    • ·
      Template-driven check: section X exists? Content X has substance?
    • ·
      ID consistency regex: normalize all references before check
    • ·
      Output: checklist with PASS/FAIL/CONCERN per item
  3. 03
    Architecture review
    ADRs have clear rationale, NFRs are addressed, risks have mitigation.
    arch review board
    what

    AI review Architecture: ADR quality + NFR coverage + risk register.

    • 1
      ADR quality
      • Every ADR has Context/Decision/Consequences/Alternatives complete?
      • Status field set (Proposed/Accepted)?
      • All tech stack decisions have an ADR?
    • 2
      NFR coverage
      • Each NFR from PRD has a component/pattern addressing it in the architecture?
      • 99.9% uptime NFR has HA topology?
      • Security NFR has threat model?
    • 3
      Risk register present? Top 5 risks have mitigations?
    why
    • ·
      Architecture review is often skipped — 'trust the architect'
    • ·
      Independent review catches: missing ADR (undocumented decision), under-addressed NFR
    • ·
      NFR-architecture gap = failure surprise in production
    how
    • ·
      ATAM-light: each NFR → find addressing component → rate adequacy
    • ·
      Tech stack alignment with team skills check
    • ·
      Vendor lock-in risk graded
  4. 04
    Cohesion check
    PRD ↔ Architecture: every FR has a solution, every decision is grounded in PRD.
    traceability matrix
    what

    AI run cohesion check: PRD ↔ Architecture traceability.

    • 1
      Build traceability matrix
      • Rows = FRs from PRD
      • Cols = architecture components
      • X = component addresses FR
    • 2
      2 types of issues
      • Empty row — FR has no component → architecture incomplete
      • Empty column — component not justified by any FR → over-engineering
    • 3
      Flag all gaps as Blocker if FR = Must priority
    why
    • ·
      Cohesion gap is a silent killer — PRD says 'users upload files', architecture has no FileService
    • ·
      Discovered after shipping = emergency fix, downtime risk
    • ·
      Over-engineering = wasted sprint capacity + maintenance burden
    how
    • ·
      Same traceability matrix technique as 03·B step 01
    • ·
      PO perspective: 'which feature will users miss?' → check corresponding FR row
    • ·
      ADR cross-check: does every architecture decision trace back to a PRD requirement?
  5. 05
    Epic quality review
    Validate per best practices: user value, independence, dependencies.
    INVEST validation
    what

    AI review epics: INVEST compliance + AC quality + dependency graph.

    • 1
      Per story INVEST check
      • Independent? (no hard block on adjacent story?)
      • Estimable? (can dev estimate without major uncertainty?)
      • Small? (≤3 days dev work?)
      • Testable? (≥2 AC defined?)
    • 2
      AC quality check
      • Stories with 0-1 AC → insufficient spec flag
      • Stories with >7 AC → too large, split flag
      • Gherkin format: Given/When/Then present?
    • 3
      Dependency graph cycle check: A→B→A = circular dependency = blocker
    why
    • ·
      Bad stories at this gate = bad sprint — sprint failure more expensive
    • ·
      Story without clear AC → code no one can verify
    • ·
      Circular dependency → sprint never completes
    how
    • ·
      Automated INVEST scoring: count AC, check size estimate present
    • ·
      Cycle detection: graph traversal (DFS cycle check)
    • ·
      Output: per-story scorecard with specific issues
  6. 06
    Final assessment
    Conclude: PASS, CONCERNS (minor fixes needed), or FAIL (must return to rework).
    go/no-gogate review
    what

    AI concludes with binary verdict and saves readiness report.

    • 1
      Verdict criteria
      • PASS — 0 Blockers + ≤3 Concerns → ready for Phase 4
      • NEEDS REVIEW — 0 Blockers + >3 Concerns → proceed with documented caveats
      • FAIL — ≥1 Blocker → must rework before Phase 4
    • 2
      Report structure
      • Scorecard — per-section PASS/CONCERNS/FAIL
      • Blockers — must fix list with owner
      • Concerns — should fix list with rationale
      • Verdict — go/no-go with reasoning
    why
    • ·
      Binary gate (go/no-go) is the critical output
    • ·
      Without explicit gate, project drifts into Phase 4 with planning gaps → sprint chaos
    • ·
      CONCERNS state still allows proceed but with documented risks — informed decision
    how
    • ·
      Blocker definition: issue that WILL cause Phase 4 failure if not fixed
    • ·
      Concern definition: issue that MIGHT cause problems but manageable
    • ·
      Save: readiness-report.md with timestamp and verdict

Markdown (pass/fail gate)

04
Implementation
Build one story at a time · Sprint-based loop
Required
once
Sprint Planning
SM
per story
Create Story
SM
per story

per story

per epic

SM
04 · A
Sprint Planning
Initialize and refresh tracking — parse epics, detect story statuses from file system, generate sprint-status.yaml. Preserves any existing action_items block when regenerating.
driven byDev
  1. 01
    Parse epic files
    Scan epic files, extract epic numbers and story IDs/titles from headers in body.
    epic discovery
    what

    Scan *epic*.md in {planning_artifacts}, extract epic numbers and story IDs/titles from headers.

    • 1
      Find epic files — whole doc (epics.md) or sharded (index.md + section files)
    • 2
      Extract epic numbers from headers ## Epic 1:, story IDs and titles from ### Story 1.1: Title
    • 3
      Convert story header into kebab-case key: 1-1-user-authentication
    • 4
      Build complete inventory of all epics and stories
    why
    • ·
      Sprint planning need ALL epics and stories to build complete status tracking
    how
    • ·
      Whole doc takes priority over sharded if both exist
    • ·
      Fuzzy matching: accepts epics.md, bmm-epics.md, user-stories.md
    • ·
      Story ID: replace . with -, title into kebab-case
  2. 02
    Build status structure
    Create flat development_status map with default statuses for epics, stories, retrospectives.
    flat mapdefault status
    what

    Build development_status map — each epic is add 3 type entry.

    • 1
      Epic entry: epic-{N}: backlog
    • 2
      Story entries: {epic}-{story}-{title}: backlog
    • 3
      Retrospective entry: epic-{N}-retrospective: optional
    how
    • ·
      Order: epic → stories of epic that → retrospective → epic next
    • ·
      Default backlog for epic and story; optional for retro
  3. 03
    Apply status detection
    Check story files actual, upgrade status when file exist at, never downgrade.
    file detectionnever downgrade
    what

    With each story, check {story_location}/{story-key}.md has exist at not.

    • 1
      File exist at → upgrade up less most ready-for-dev
    • 2
      Status current further along → keep original (not downgrade)
    • 3
      File not exist at, doesn't have status → keep backlog
    how
    • ·
      Check path: {story_location}/{story-key}.md
    • ·
      Compare with existing status in sprint-status.yaml if the file already exists, take the further-along status
  4. 04
    Generate sprint-status.yaml
    Ghi flat development_status map with metadata header ra file.
    artifact
    what

    Ghi sprint-status.yaml with flat development_status map and metadata header.

    how
    • ·
      Output path: {implementation_artifacts}/sprint-status.yaml
    • ·
      Metadata output current 2 time: form comment (#) to read, and form YAML key:value to parse
  5. 05
    Validate + Report
    Check completeness and report totals before concluding.
    validation
    what

    Validate completeness and report totals.

    • 1
      Validate: every epic/story/retro has entry, has no entry extra, status values valid rate, YAML syntax correct
    • 2
      Report totals: epic count, story count, in-progress count, done count
    why
    • ·
      Artifacts drive state — YAML persists across sessions, every agent read together a source
Skills
bmad-sprint-planning
Frameworks
Flat status mapFile-based status detectionNever-downgrade ruleStatus state machine
Format
YAML sprint-status.yaml
IN
*epic*.md story files sprint-status.yaml
OUT
sprint-status.yaml
04 · B
Create Story
Create story file entire aspect — exhaustive artifact analysis, architecture guardrails, web research latest tech — to dev agent has every order need for flawless implementation. Quick-dev subagent usage wording clarified.
driven byDev
  1. 01
    Determine target story
    User provides story path or auto-discover entry first has status backlog in sprint-status.yaml.
    story selection
    what

    Determine story will is create: user provides story path (vd: 1-2, epic 2 story 4) or auto-discover from sprint-status.yaml.

    • ·
      User provides → parse into story_key
    • ·
      Auto-discover: read FULL sprint-status.yaml (ALL lines, don't skip) → find entry first match pattern N-N-name with status=backlog
    • ·
      If is story first in epic: update epic status backlog → in-progress
    • ·
      Halt if has no backlog story
    why
    • ·
      Just-in-time: create-story run each story, not bulk-prep
    • ·
      Bulk prep upfront → stories after stale because learnings change context
    • ·
      ZERO USER INTERVENTION: fully automated except step choose story initial
    how
    • ·
      Read FULL sprint-status.yaml — without skipping any lines
    • ·
      Filter entries match pattern N-N-name → take entry first has status=backlog
    • ·
      Halt with message clear if not find see backlog story
  2. 02
    Load and analyze core artifacts
    Load epics, prd, architecture, ux (SELECTIVE_LOAD); read previous story file and analyze git commits recent most.
    previous story intelligencegit intelligence
    what

    Load all core artifacts necessary and analyze context from previous work.

    • ·
      Load: epics (*epic*.md), prd (*prd*.md), architecture (*architecture*.md), ux (*ux*.md) — SELECTIVE_LOAD
    • ·
      If story_num > 1: load previous story file → extract dev notes, review feedback, code patterns established, problems encountered
    • ·
      Analyze 1-5 git commits recent most: files modified, patterns, dependencies, test approaches
    why
    • ·
      Previous story intelligence avoid reinventing wheels and repeat again mistakes
    • ·
      Git intelligence for know patterns and dependencies is being use actual
    • ·
      SELECTIVE_LOAD keep context window focused — not dump the entire artifacts
    how
    • ·
      Load epics the entire, the file different only load sections related to story
    • ·
      Previous story: read dev notes and review feedback — extract patterns already is approve
    • ·
      Git: git log --oneline -5 → determine files is being modify many most
  3. 03
    Architecture analysis for developer guardrails
    Extract tech stack, naming conventions, API patterns, DB schemas, security requirements; read completely the files is mark mark UPDATE.
    architecture guardrailsregression prevention
    what

    Analyze architecture to create guardrails that prevent the dev agent from going down the wrong path.

    • ·
      Extract: tech stack, code structure, naming conventions, API patterns, DB schemas, security requirements, performance requirements, testing standards, deployment patterns, integration patterns
    • ·
      Identify files marked UPDATE (not NEW) → read each file completely
    • ·
      Document: current state / what story changes / what must be preserved
    why
    • ·
      Skipping read UPDATE files is primary cause cause implementation failures and review cycles
    • ·
      Architecture guardrails prevent common LLM mistakes: wrong libraries, wrong file locations, breaking regressions
    • ·
      Story implementation must leave system working end-to-end — without breaking existing functionality
    how
    • ·
      EXHAUSTIVE ANALYSIS REQUIRED: read complete, don't skim
    • ·
      Read The entire each file UPDATE — not only headers or summaries
    • ·
      Document clearly: file X existing what, story will change what, part any must keep unchanged
  4. 04
    Web research for latest technical specifics
    Research latest stable versions, breaking changes, security vulnerabilities, deprecations and best practices for libraries/frameworks in story.
    web researchlatest tech
    what

    Identify libraries/frameworks from architecture analysis, then web search to get the latest technical information.

    • ·
      Latest stable version of each relevant library
    • ·
      Breaking changes, security vulnerabilities, deprecations
    • ·
      Best practices and migration notes current
    why
    • ·
      LLM training data can outdated — web research ensure dev use API correct, not deprecated
    • ·
      Security vulnerabilities in dependencies = risk if not check
    • ·
      Breaking changes in minor/patch versions can cause runtime errors
    how
    • ·
      Web search each library: "library-name latest version changelog"
    • ·
      Include in story: specific versions, API endpoints, security patches, migration notes
    • ·
      Only research libraries directly related to story — not research the entire stack
  5. 05
    Create comprehensive story file
    Init from template, write complete all sections, set Status = ready-for-dev. Questions is save for end — without interrupting the workflow.
    story fileready-for-dev
    what

    Total valid all analysis from step 1-4 into story file entire aspect at {implementation_artifacts}/{story_key}.md.

    • ·
      Init from template.md; write: story requirements, developer context, technical requirements, architecture compliance
    • ·
      Include: library/framework requirements, file structure, testing requirements
    • ·
      Include (if has): previous story intelligence, git intelligence, latest tech info from web research
    • ·
      Set Status = ready-for-dev; SAVE QUESTIONS for end — without interrupting the workflow
    why
    • ·
      Story file = self-contained handoff artifact — dev agent doesn't need re-read PRD/architecture/epics
    • ·
      ZERO USER INTERVENTION: not ask user midway, automatically decision and note questions for end
    • ·
      Comprehensive context = flawless implementation, less review cycles more
    how
    • ·
      Path: {implementation_artifacts}/{story_key}.md
    • ·
      Init from template — don't write from scratch; fill in each section in order
    • ·
      If has sentence ask: note into part end story, without stopping ask user
  6. 06
    Update sprint status and finalize
    Validate story file, update sprint-status.yaml (status → ready-for-dev, last_updated), preserve ALL comments and structure, report completion.
    validationstatus update
    what

    Validate story file and update sprint-status.yaml to signal the story is ready.

    • ·
      Validate story file against checklist.md → apply fixes if needed
    • ·
      Load FULL sprint-status.yaml, find story_key, verify status=backlog
    • ·
      Update: status → ready-for-dev, update last_updated
    • ·
      Save: preserve ALL comments and structure; report completion
    why
    • ·
      Validation ensure story file complete before dispatch — not to dev agent discover missing info
    • ·
      Status ready-for-dev (not in-progress) — signal clear story already prepared, not yet started
    • ·
      Preserve comments in YAML — contain context important for team members different
    how
    • ·
      Checklist validation: each required section present, has no placeholder text
    • ·
      YAML update: only change status and last_updated — without rewriting the whole file
    • ·
      Report: story name, path, summary of the sections created

04 · C
Dev Story
Implement story from story file — auto-discover story, RED-GREEN-REFACTOR each task related continue without stopping, validate DoD, hand off for code review.
driven byDev
  1. 01
    Find & load story
    Auto-discover story first status=ready-for-dev in sprint-status.yaml, or use story path user provides; read FULL story file.
    story discoverysprint-status
    what

    Determine story need implement and read the entire content.

    • ·
      User provides story path → use directly
    • ·
      Has no path → read FULL sprint-status.yaml → find entry first N-N-name has status=ready-for-dev
    • ·
      Read COMPLETE story file: AC, Tasks/Subtasks, Dev Notes, Dev Agent Record, File List, Change Log, Status
    • ·
      Load project-context.md if exist at
    • ·
      HALT if not find is story or story file not accessible
    why
    • ·
      Story file is single source of truth — every implementation decision comes from here
    • ·
      Dev Notes contain architecture guardrails, previous learnings from create-story — not reading them means missing important context
    how
    • ·
      Read FULL sprint-status.yaml (ALL lines, don't skip) → filter N-N-name → take entry first status=ready-for-dev
    • ·
      If has no ready-for-dev → ask user: run create-story, validate-create-story, or provides story path
  2. 02
    Detect review continuation
    Check view story already via code review not yet — if has, extract pending follow-up items to prioritize process reason.
    review continuation
    what
    • ·
      Scan story file: has section "Senior Developer Review (AI)" not?
    • ·
      If has → review_continuation = true: extract review outcome, count unchecked [AI-Review] items, save danh policy pending
    • ·
      If not → review_continuation = false: fresh start
    why
    • ·
      Review follow-ups must is process reason before regular tasks — reviewer already identify issues specific
    • ·
      Not detecting = can skip over high-severity review findings
    how
    • ·
      Scan "Review Follow-ups (AI)" subsection in Tasks/Subtasks
    • ·
      Count: High / Med / Low severity items still unchecked
  3. 03
    Mark in-progress
    Capture baseline_commit into story YAML frontmatter; update sprint-status.yaml: ready-for-dev → in-progress.
    baseline commitstate tracking
    what
    • ·
      Only perform if status current = ready-for-dev And story YAML doesn't have baseline_commit
    • ·
      Run git rev-parse HEAD → save into YAML frontmatter of story: baseline_commit: <sha>
    • ·
      Update sprint-status.yaml: story_key → in-progress, update last_updated
    why
    • ·
      baseline_commit lets code-review know which commit the diff should start from
    • ·
      Status in-progress signals to the team that the story is being implemented
    how
    • ·
      If git not available → baseline_commit: NO_VCS
    • ·
      If YAML frontmatter already has baseline_commit → Do not overwrite
    • ·
      Update sprint-status.yaml: FULL file, preserve ALL comments and structure
  4. 04
    Implement task — RED·GREEN·REFACTOR loop
    Repeat again for each task: write failing tests (RED) → implement minimal code (GREEN) → refactor → validate → mark [x] → next task. Without stopping midway.
    TDDred-green-refactorzero-interrupt
    what

    Loop repeat per-task following order Tasks/Subtasks in story file:

    • ·
      RED: write failing tests → confirm tests Fail before code
    • ·
      GREEN: implement minimal code to tests pass — not add what outside task scope
    • ·
      REFACTOR: improve code structure, tests still xanh after refactor
    • ·
      Validate: run full test suite, check AC related to task
    • ·
      Mark task [x] Only when all tests actually exist at and pass 100%
    • ·
      If review_continuation = true: process reason [AI-Review] items before regular tasks
    why
    • ·
      TDD force articulate behavior before choose implementation — avoid over-engineering
    • ·
      NEVER stop because "milestones", "significant progress", or "session boundaries" — only HALT when has thing event specific
    • ·
      NEVER mark [x] if tests not actually pass — NO LYING OR CHEATING
    how
    • ·
      HALT if: need add dependency outside story spec, 3 consecutive implementation failures, missing required config
    • ·
      Update File List after each task: ALL new/modified/deleted files (relative paths)
    • ·
      Ghi completion notes into Dev Agent Record after each task complete
  5. 05
    Story completion & DoD validation
    Verify all tasks [x], run full regression, validate Definition of Done, update status: in-progress → review.
    definition of doneregression
    what

    Gate end before story into review queue:

    • ·
      Re-scan the entire story document: verify ALL tasks and subtasks already [x]
    • ·
      Run full regression suite — don't skip
    • ·
      DoD checklist: all AC satisfied, unit/integration/e2e tests added, linting pass, File List complete, Dev Agent Record has notes, Change Log has summary
    • ·
      Update story Status → review
    • ·
      Update sprint-status.yaml: story_key → review, preserve ALL comments
    why
    • ·
      DoD validation before signal review = not wasting reviewer time with incomplete stories
    • ·
      Status review (not done) — wait code-review approve before done
    how
    • ·
      HALT if: any any task not yet [x], regression failures, File List missing, DoD fail
    • ·
      YAML update: only change status and last_updated — without rewriting the whole file
  6. 06
    Communication & handoff
    Report completion, explain per user_skill_level, suggest next step: run code-review with different LLM.
    handoff
    what
    • ·
      Summary: story ID, story key, title, key changes, tests added, files modified
    • ·
      Provides story file path and current status (review)
    • ·
      Ask user has need explain what not (per user_skill_level)
    • ·
      Suggest next steps: run code-review, verify ACs, check sprint status
    why
    • ·
      User need know story already done and can has questions about implementation decisions
    • ·
      Code-review should use different LLM to avoid blind spots from together model already implemented
    how
    • ·
      Tailor explanation per user_skill_level — junior need e.g., senior need trade-offs
    • ·
      Tip: "For best results, run code-review using a different LLM than the one that implemented this story"
Skills
